meta.name: At the door
meta.open: Someone is knocking. You are on the inside of your own front door.
meta.law: A warrant signed by an immigration officer does not let anyone in. Only a judge can sign one that does — and they almost never do.

forcedEntry.text: They came through the door. You never opened it, you never consented, and the paper they carried was signed by an immigration officer, not a judge. Write down the time. Write down what it said. A lawyer starts there.

credits.label: Asked if a judge signed it
Asked them to slide it under the door

responses.speaker: agent
texts: Federal agents. Open the door.
We know you are in there. Open up.

answers.text: Stay where you are. Talk through the door.
Open it a crack, just to see who it is.
Open the door. Refusing looks guilty.
answers.why: You let them in. Nothing after that was a search. It was a visit you agreed to.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: He puts a boot in the gap. “Since it is already open—”
A hand is on the door now. “Just step back, we will come to you.”

answers.text: Push it shut. “I did not consent to that.”
Step back and let him follow you in.
answers.why: A boot in a doorway is not a search warrant. But you treated it like an invitation, and that is all it takes.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: We have a warrant.
There is a warrant. Open up.

answers.text: “Is it signed by a judge?”
Ask who they are looking for.
“Then come in.”
answers.why: You said yes. Consent is the only key they needed.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: I told you. It has your name on it.
It is a federal warrant. That is all you need to know.

answers.text: “Slide it under the door, or show me in the window.”
Tell them your name so they can check.
Open the door far enough to look at it.
answers.why: The door came open. From there it was never going to close.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: “Thank you. Now open the door, this is easier for you.”
“Good. Confirmed. Open up.”

answers.text: “Confirming a name is not consent. I still do not consent.”
“Fine. You have my name, open up.”
answers.why: You gave them a fact and then gave them the door. The name was never the leverage — your next word was.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: Last chance. Open it or we open it for you. Do not make this hard.
Every minute you stall goes in the report. Open the door.
He holds the paper up to the window. The header reads DEPARTMENT OF HOMELAND SECURITY. At the bottom: Field Office Director. No judge ever saw it.

answers.text: “I do not consent to entry.”
Tell them nobody is home.
Unlock it. They said they would break it.
answers.why: They did not break it. You opened it.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: “We can hear the TV. Open the door.”
“Someone is in there. Open it now.”

answers.text: Say nothing further.
“Okay, fine, I am home.” Open the door.
answers.why: The lie bought you nothing and cost you credibility. Silence would have cost you nothing either — and given them nothing to catch.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: There. Warrant. Now open the door.
You have seen it. Open the door.

answers.text: “That is not signed by a judge. I do not consent.”
“That is my name. I am the one you want.”
It looks official. Unlock the door.
answers.why: It said Department of Homeland Security at the top. A judge never saw it.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: Where were you born?
You got papers in there? Just tell me where you were born.

answers.text: Say nothing.
“I have lived here twelve years.”
Answer the question.

responses.speaker: agent
texts: Come out here and we will clear this up in five minutes.
Step outside. It is easier for everyone.

answers.text: “I want a lawyer. I am not opening the door.”
“Come back with a warrant a judge signed.”
Step outside.
answers.why: On the porch you are not in your home. The porch was the whole plan.
